title: On the Arithmetic of Genus 4 Curves
speaker: Jiahui Gao
abstract: We propose and study a new arithmetic invariant of non-hyperelliptic genus-4 curves: a canonical “quadratic” point on the Jacobian, defined by the two natural degree-2 maps to projective lines. Building on Xue’s result, that this point is generically non-torsion, we introduce a height-theoretic notion of bigness for families of curves in the moduli space of genus four curves, and give a criterion, via dimensions of modular quotients, for when it holds. We then exhibit two concrete 3- and 4-parameter families (the bi-involutions locus and a CM example) in which the canonical point is provably big, from which we deduce the finiteness of low-height curves and non-torsion at transcendental moduli. Our methods combine adelic Arakelov intersection theory with a generic Betti-rank argument.

proof_stages.label: Nonhyperelliptic genus-4 model and canonical quadratic point
proof_stages.summary: The talk restricts to nonhyperelliptic genus-4 curves whose canonical image is a complete intersection on a smooth quadric \(\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^1\), and defines the canonical quadratic divisor class \(\xi_C\) over the quadratic field splitting the two rulings.

proof_stages.label: Bigness and Northcott motivation
proof_stages.summary: After recalling a generic non-torsion result, the speaker defines bigness of \(\xi\) by a Néron--Tate height lower bound on an open subset and explains that it implies a Northcott-type finiteness statement for bounded degree and height.

proof_stages.label: Dimension criterion for bigness
proof_stages.summary: For a section of an abelian scheme, the speaker introduces the minimal abelian subscheme through which it is torsion-translated and states/proves a criterion saying bigness is equivalent to inequalities \(\dim S\le \dim \phi_D(S)+\dim B\) for all relevant abelian subquotients.

proof_stages.label: Triple-involution family and elliptic quotient decomposition
proof_stages.summary: A three-dimensional family of bidegree-(3,3) curves stable under three involutions is constructed, its genus-2 and elliptic quotients are computed to split the Jacobian into four elliptic factors with non-torsion projections of \(\xi\), and the dimension criterion is checked to prove bigness on this family.

proof_stages.label: Order-15 automorphism family and simplicity argument
proof_stages.summary: Using the special curve with \(\zeta_{15}\)-action, together with a non-torsion specialization and the simplicity of the special Jacobian, the speaker reduces the criterion to the cases \(B=0\) and \(B=A\) and obtains bigness for families of dimension at most four.

examples.name: Displayed (3,3)-curve with torsion quadratic point
examples.note: The first explicitly written curve on \(\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^1\) is used only to make concrete that the canonical quadratic point \(\xi_C\) can specialize to a torsion point.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes down the explicit curve and states that the associated quadratic point is torsion, but does not compute or verify the torsion property on it.

examples.name: Displayed curve with non-torsion quadratic point
examples.note: The second explicitly written bidegree-(3,3) curve is offered as a concrete specialization where \(\xi_C\) is non-torsion, emphasizing that such examples are not obvious to find directly.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker displays the explicit curve and states that its quadratic point is non-torsion, but gives no calculation or check for this particular curve.

examples.name: Related triple-cover family \(C_{\lambda,\mu,\nu}\)
examples.note: The family roughly of the form \(y^3=x(x-1)(x-\lambda)(x-\mu)(x-\nu)\) is presented as a related conjectural family in the same geometric cone, not as a step in proving the stated bigness theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: For the family C_{lambda,mu,nu}, the speaker writes its equation and then checks its geometric placement by computing/listing differential forms and using them to see it lies in the cone/section.
